question: How do mapping instructions control event subscriptions for catch events during a process instance migration?

options.text: A mapped source catch event has its subscription migrated; an unmapped source catch event has its subscription closed; an unmapped target catch event has a new subscription opened.
All catch event subscriptions are recreated at migration time, regardless of mapping; mappings only affect non-event elements.
Subscriptions for unmapped target catch events stay dormant until the process instance later reaches them; they are not opened during migration.
An unmapped source catch event keeps its subscription open so the instance can still receive the corresponding event after migration.

a.text: The docs describe three outcomes driven by catch-event mapping instructions: migrate (mapped), remove (unmapped source closes the subscription), and add (unmapped target opens a new subscription).

b.text: Subscriptions are not blindly recreated. The mapping plan determines whether a subscription is migrated, closed, or opened.

c.text: A target catch event without an incoming mapping causes a new subscription to be opened during migration, not later when the instance reaches it.

d.text: If a source catch event is not mapped, its subscription is closed during migration. It does not remain open after migration.

explanation: Mapping instructions on catch events drive the subscription lifecycle during migration. If a source catch event is mapped, the existing subscription is migrated. If a source catch event has no mapping, the subscription is closed. If a target catch event is not the target of any mapping, a new subscription is opened.
